original_abstract: Almost all current dependency parsers classify based on millions of sparse indicator features.Not only do these features generalize poorly, but the cost of feature computation restricts parsing speed significantly.In this work, we propose a novel way of learning a neural network classifier for use in a greedy, transition-based dependency parser.Because this classifier learns and uses just a small number of dense features, it can work very fast, while achieving an about 2% improvement in unlabeled and labeled attachment scores on both English and Chinese datasets.Concretely, our parser is able to parse more than 1000 sentences per second at 92.2% unlabeled attachment score on the English Penn Treebank.
humanized_abstract: Current state-of-the-art dependency parsers classify based on indicator features, and they generally have millions of such features. These features tend to have poor generalization and feature extraction is a bottleneck in the parsing algorithm. In this paper, we design a transition-based parser using a neural network classifier that is based on a small set of dense features and achieves both higher attachment scores (2% increase in both UAS and LAS on WSJ and Penn Treebank for English and PCCT for Chinese) and higher parsing speed (capable of parsing 1000 sentences per second with 92.2% UAS on WSJ.

document.output: Current state-of-the-art dependency parsers classify based on indicator features, and they generally have millions of such features. In this paper, we design a transition-based parser using a neural network classifier that is based on a small set of dense features and achieves both higher attachment scores (2% increase in both UAS and LAS on WSJ and Penn Treebank for English and PCCT for Chinese) and higher parsing speed (capable of parsing 1000 sentences per second with 92.2% UAS on WSJ.
